quote: Yeah. So it's gonna be under my my mom and dad's name. I live here with them, so the the entire program will be under their name.
stewart_read: Brandon is the adult child, not the homeowner of record. The schema is explicit: co-decision-makers on the title must be present at the appointment. The rep acknowledged the disclosure but did not apply the protocol — no attempt to confirm whether the parents would be on the Saturday call.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have been waiting to see if Brandon would self-resolve the authority question before pressing — which he did at 05:33. Rejected: Brandon's self-resolution ('technically it is my house') is not title confirmation, and the rep never returned to the parents' role after 05:33.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask the rep: 'If the parents are on the title and the bill is in their name, what happens when the closer shows up Saturday and they need a signature?' Walk through protocols.adult_child_decision_maker. The correct move was to ask at 02:03 whether the parents would be available Saturday — not to wait for Brandon to self-declare.

quote: Just just put mine for now if that's okay. Okay. Let me proceed from there.
stewart_read: This is the moment the protocol failure crystallized. Brandon is explicitly asking the rep to proceed under his name despite having just disclosed the title is in his parents' names. The rep said 'K' and moved on. The schema requires the rep to ensure title holders are present at the appointment — accepting 'just put mine for now' bypasses that requirement entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: the rep may not have recognized that 'just put mine for now' was a soft instruction to ignore the ownership question. Accepted: there is no evidence of a strategic reason to defer here.
coaching_implication: This is the specific line to replay. The rep needed a single bridging question: 'Totally — and just so our specialist is prepared, will your parents be around Saturday too, or is it just you?' That question costs nothing and protects the sit.

stewart_read: The rep captured the kWh number efficiently off a paper bill — good pivot given the mail-bill situation. But per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture alone is not sufficient. The pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' The rep got the number and moved on to residence length. No emotional alignment against the utility, no curiosity created around what that usage means in dollars. The bill anchor was collected as data, not deployed as leverage.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have been planning to use the bill number later in the pitch. Rejected: the rep never returned to the 1348 kWh figure after this point.
coaching_implication: Per analysis_directives.bill_anchor_depth, show the rep the capture-and-drop pattern. The suggested move after '1348' is not a number fabrication — it's an emotional bridge: something like 'That's a solid amount of usage — that's exactly the kind of home where the math tends to work in your favor. Let me make sure we get you an accurate design.' The flip doesn't require inventing savings figures.

quote: I have a Tesla phone. I have a Tesla, so it's gonna be a little bit more.
stewart_read: Brandon volunteers that he has a Tesla (vehicle) — an unsolicited signal that his electric load is higher than the 1348 kWh baseline suggests. This is a qualification-strengthening detail: EV ownership means higher ongoing load, which improves the solar economics and system sizing case. The rep said 'Okay. Okay.' and moved on. The signal was heard but not anchored.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have mentally noted the EV for the specialist's design. Possible, but there is no evidence the rep flagged it for the handoff or used it to deepen Brandon's curiosity about system sizing.
coaching_implication: This is a lead-quality signal the rep walked past. In the 1-on-1, ask: 'What does a Tesla EV mean for the system size we'd design?' Then show how anchoring on it — 'That actually changes the design conversation, because we'd want to size the system to cover your driving too' — creates curiosity and strengthens the appointment's value proposition before the closer even shows up.

quote: I'll just forward you the bill that I forwarded to the last company that I worked with.
stewart_read: Brandon casually discloses he already shared his bill with a prior company (Freedom Pros, which filed bankruptcy). This is a competitive intelligence signal per verify.prior_designs — Brandon has been through at least one prior solar evaluation. The rep said 'Okay. Sounds great. Thank you.' and moved on. The schema item asks whether the customer has seen a prior design; this confirms they have. The rep missed the opportunity to surface what Brandon saw, what he liked or didn't, and whether he has a design to compare against.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep may have judged that pressing on the prior design would slow the bill-collection momentum. Plausible, but the question takes ten seconds and surfaces competitive context the closer needs.
coaching_implication: Add a single follow-up question here: 'Did they get you a full design before things fell through?' If yes, the closer walks in knowing Brandon has a reference point. If no, the closer is the first full proposal — different framing. Either way, the closer needs to know.

quote: Awesome. Yeah. Because I got me and my bunch of my neighbors, that were installed. We were gonna go with, you know, Aurora solar or something like that. But, apparently, they just filed bankruptcy. Freedom Pro. Sorry. Freedom Pro.
stewart_read: Brandon volunteers a neighborhood solar cluster — multiple households that were actively moving toward solar together before the provider collapsed. This is a multi-household referral signal hiding inside a competitor-bankruptcy disclosure. The rep caught the referral angle (01:36 referral program mention) but framed it as a future benefit rather than anchoring on the immediate urgency: Brandon and his neighbors are actively looking for a replacement provider right now.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep's referral mention was a reasonable response to the neighbor signal. Accepted as partial credit — the rep heard the signal. The gap is in urgency framing, not in missing the signal entirely.
coaching_implication: The neighbor cluster is a same-day urgency signal, not a future referral opportunity. In the 1-on-1, ask the rep: 'How many households do you think were in that group?' Then show how anchoring on the urgency — 'So you and your neighbors are all looking right now, and the company you were going with just went under — that's actually perfect timing for us to step in' — changes the energy of the pitch without fabricating anything.

quote: Can you can you ping me as soon as you're on the way so that I know your ETA, and then I I can make sure I'm on home accordingly?
stewart_read: Brandon's ETA-ping request is not a fragility signal on its own — it's a reasonable logistical ask from someone who wants to be home. The real button-up gaps are what the rep did not complete: no explicit one-hour block stated, no 'not driving, at a computer' anchor, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake per button_up required elements. The rep confirmed the appointment and promised the specialist would ping, but the structural confirmation protocol was incomplete.
alternative_hypothesis: The ETA request could indicate Brandon is not fully committed to being home. Rejected: Brandon's overall energy at close is high ('You're the best,' 'Looking forward talking to you soon') — the ETA ask reads as logistics, not hedging.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through button_up required elements. The missing beats — one-hour block, computer/not-driving anchor, 'is that fair' handshake — are each independently predictive of no-show rate per the schema. On a Saturday in-home appointment, the 'at a computer' anchor is especially relevant since the specialist will likely want to show a design.
